data.title: Athene Holding: A 7.1% Yielding, Investment-Grade Addition To Our Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored by Arbitrage Trader.

Overview

In our recent articles, we are showing you the following chart:

This is essential to our idea that the current equity is overvalued. It's time for us to reorientate ourselves and relocate our portfolio to a heavier fixed-income exposure. The fixed-income securities are inherently safer investments than equity, and when offering a higher yield is an opportunity hard for us to pass.

The investment idea we want to present to your attention is the Athene Holding Ltd. Rate Reset Junior Subordinated Debentures due 03/30/2064 (NYSE:ATHS), issued by Athene Holding Ltd., a subsidiary of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO).

The Issue

The prospectus contains all the relevant information about the security. Here, we will provide only the most important metrics used in the evaluation process.

ATHS will pay a 7.25% annual interest in the form of $0.4531 quarterly distributions until its first possible redemption date on 3/30/2029. After that, the annual interest will be equal to the five-year US Treasury rate plus a fixed spread of 2.986%, resetting every five years. The stated maturity date for the security is 3/30/2064. ATHS was credit-rated BBB by S&P, Baa2 by Moody's, and BBB- by Fitch at the date of its IPO.

The daily price chart of the issue looks like this:

Currently, ATHS is trading at a $25.54 price with a Yield-To-Call metric of 7.09%. If not called, the bond will reset its interest on 3/30/2029 and every five years after that with the 2.986% spread over the five-year Treasuries at that time. Given the fact that there's no way for us to know what the forward yield curve in general and the five-year Treasuries, in particular, will look like for such a long period as 2064 presents, we cannot give a fair estimation of the yield-to-maturity metric for ATHS.

The forward yield curve depicted above gives us an expected value of 4.74% for the five-year Treasury yield at the end of March 2029, when the first call date/interest adjustment for ATHS will be. Based on this, if the security is not redeemed, its annual interest will be adjusted to 7.73%, or around 0.50% higher than the fixed rate of 7.25% at the moment. At the moment, the expectation for the five-year Treasury yield in 2034 is to be higher than at the reset date in 2029. The expected trend is clear and should something completely unexpected happen to the yield curve, we anticipate ATHS to trade to its call date as a low-duration debt issue.

The Company

Despite being fully owned by Apollo Global Management since the beginning of 2022, Athene Holding Ltd. issues its independent financial reports, which we can study to determine to what extent the company's financial state indicates some potential trouble for its outstanding debt.

Athene Holding Ltd., a financial services company, specializes in issuing, reinsuring, and acquiring retirement savings products for individuals and institutions in the United States and internationally. It offers fixed indexed, fixed rate, registered index-linked, private placement variable, income riders to fixed, payout, and group annuities. The company also provides life and other products that include life insurance policies assumed through reinsurance transactions and other retail products, including legacy run-off or ceded business and statutory closed blocks. In addition, it issues funding agreements to institutional investors; and offers a suite of services for investment portfolio, including direct investment management, asset allocation, mergers and acquisitions asset diligence, and operational support services, including investment compliance, tax, legal, and risk management support. Athene Holding Ltd. distributes its products through various channels that include retail, flow reinsurance, institutional, and acquisitions and block reinsurance. The company was incorporated in 2008 and is headquartered in West Des Moines, Iowa.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

Distributions

The table contains information about how the net income of Athene Holding has been distributed in the company for the last five quarters with SEC filings, and also the Interest Payments for the same periods. The trailing twelve months' $473 million in interest payments represent around 9% of the $5.2 billion of net income attributable to the common shareholders and only 6% of the $7.6 billion total net income for the same time frame. If the company remains in its current state of operations, its creditors should feel safe enough about their interest payments, as the cushion for them is large enough. Moreover - there was not one of the last five quarters, in which the net income could not cover the interest payments, and that shows consistency of the good kind.

Capital Structure

Below is Athene Holding's capital structure as of the third quarter report filing for 2024. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is presented, too, for the sake of comparison in the short term.

As of Q3 '24, Athene Holding Ltd. had a total debt of $5.7 billion, which was senior to the preferred equity of $3.2 billion. The common equity of $14.3 billion is the lowest position in the capital structure. We can calculate the debt-to-equity ratio at 0.33. This means that as of the last quarterly filing, Athene has enough equity to cover three times its outstanding debt. This is what we want to see in a company's capital structure when we consider investing in some of its debt issues. ATHS is a junior subordinated debenture, meaning it is with the least priority of all the outstanding debt issues of the company. Nevertheless, the equity cushion is large enough so even the junior debt of the company is well covered.

Credit Rating

This is the beginning of the credit action commentary in which Fitch Ratings affirms the 'A-' Long-Term Issuer Default Rating of Athene Holding Ltd. with a Stable Outlook. This is what the summary of the credit scores of the big rating agencies looks like for Athene:

Athene Holding Ltd.'s OTC bond issues

Athene Holding has quite several OTC bond issues, with various credit ratings and maturity dates. Above we are showing the yield curve of the company's OTC debt issues Baa1-rated by Moody's as they are the ones closest to the Baa2-rated listed issue ATHS. The issues with the shortest duration from this set trade with yields-to-worst of around 5.2%. Keeping in mind that they are a credit notch higher than ATHS, we can assess that the exchange-listed security will be fairly priced to its OTC brothers if it has a YTC of around 5.5% or a price of $27.

Athene Holding Ltd.'s exchange-listed securities

Besides ATHS Athene Holding has five more issues listed on the exchange. However, all of them are preferred stocks, so not exactly the most suitable comparisons to the debt issue we are looking into.

Peers Comparison

Insurance Sector

The chart below contains the exchange-listed debt issues from the Insurance sector, trading above par value before their respective call date, that we deem comparable by their yield-to-call metric. The issues are investment-grade rated by at least one of the three big rating agencies.

The same securities with all the relevant metrics:

Debt issues trading above par

The chart below contains the exchange-listed debt issues from all the sectors, trading above par value before their respective call date, that we deem comparable by their yield-to-call metric. Again, the issues are investment-grade rated by at least one of the three big rating agencies.

The same securities with all the relevant metrics:

Optional Redemption

We may elect to redeem the debentures:

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Tax Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption;

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Tax Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption;

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Rating Agency Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to 102% of the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption.

Source: ATHS Prospectus

Conclusion

ATHS is a Fixed-Rate Reset junior subordinated debenture of Athene Holding, and based on the five-year Treasury forward yield and its current pricing, we expect it to trade based on its yield-to-call metric as a short-term duration vehicle. The issue is relatively undervalued to the short-term OTC debt issues of the company and exchange-traded peers.

Athene Holding Ltd.'s strong financial state and low-leveraged capital structure raise the attractiveness of its debt issues. However, the fact that the common share is not listed can be viewed as a problem by some, as we cannot check how the market evaluates it.

Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund.

Returns or yields for the fund would have been lower if a portion of the management fee had not been waived. Review the annual or semiannual report for the most current information.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Munis retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the quarter. However, municipal bonds(munis) generally fared better than the taxable fixed-income market. A third-quarter rally, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s easing campaign, helped most bond sectors, including munis, maintain one-year gains.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved, and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Municipal bond yields tracked Treasury yields higher during the quarter, but at a slower pace, and most muni-to-Treasury ratios rose. Overall, munis outperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation(GO) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Supply slowed in the quarter, grew for the year. As expected, fourth-quarter municipal bond supply shrunk compared with the third quarter’s level, as many issuers pushed deals forward to avoid presidential election-triggered volatility. For the year, total muni supply increased 29% compared with 2023, according to Barclays.

Fundamentals remained stable. While municipal credit trends continued to level off, balance sheets for most state and local entities remained fairly robust. Fiscal 2025 budgets for most U.S. states include modest revenue growth and conservative spending plans alongside strong reserve balances and liquidity. Additionally, rating upgrades slightly outpaced downgrades, and defaults remained rare.

High-yield munis outperformed. Muni credit spreads tightened modestly for the quarter. High-yield municipal bonds declined but outperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. For the year, high-yield munis significantly outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Duration detracted. Our longer-than-index duration position weighed on the portfolio’s performance compared with the benchmark as yields rose. Our positioning along the yield curve, which favored short and intermediate maturities, helped offset some negative duration effects.

Sector allocations aided results. Our sector allocations broadly contributed to results. Underweight positions relative to the index in state GO bonds and prerefunded securities and an out-of-index stake in hospitals were top contributors. Those allocations more than offset modest negative effects from underweights in the special tax and toll facilities sectors.

Security selection contributed. Our security choices lifted results compared with the benchmark, primarily in the special tax, water and sewer and local GO bond sectors. Our selections among charter schools and toll facilities weighed on relative performance.

Positioning for the Future

Growth may gather momentum. We expect slower growth in early 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, may weaken consumer demand and employment. By 2025’s second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may reignite growth. As the Trump administration's tax policy unfolds, we’ll monitor the potential muni market effects.

Credit backdrop to moderate. We expect previously strong credit trends to continue moderating. However, healthy reserves and sound liquidity should help mitigate the effects of slowing revenue growth and waning federal stimulus funds. We also expect an increase in state and local governments facing larger projected budget gaps, such as those challenging Chicago and Oakland, California.

Focused on quality. Given our expectations for growth to slow, we are maintaining a bias toward higher-quality securities and a slight duration overweight. As always, stringent security selection guides our process. With valuations tight and political priorities yet to unfold, we’re focused on select sectors, waiting for better opportunities to meaningfully boost credit exposure.

Data presented reflects past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. To obtain performance data current to the most recent month end, please visit www.americancentury.com/performance. Investment return and share value will fluctuate, and redemption value may be more or less than original cost. Data assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. For information about other share classes available, please consult the prospectus. There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Dividends and yields represent past performance and there is no guarantee that they will continue to be paid.

You should consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before you invest. The fund’s prospectus or summary prospectus, which can be obtained at American Century Investments® Home, contains this and other information about the fund, and should be read carefully before investing.

Investment return and principal value of security investments will fluctuate. The value at the time of redemption may be more or less than the original cost. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The opinions expressed are those of the portfolio investment team and are no guarantee of the future performance of any American Century Investments portfolio. Statements regarding specific holdings represent personal views and compensation has not been received in connection with such views. This information is for an educational purpose only and is not intended to serve as investment advice.

The information is not intended as a personalized recommendation or fiduciary advice and should not be relied upon for investment, accounting, legal or tax advice.

Interest rate changes are among the most significant factors affecting bond return. When rates decline, bond prices rise and the fund may generate less income. When rates rise, bond prices fall. Depending on your tax situation, investment income may be subject to state and local taxes and the federal alternative minimum tax.

Investment returns are exempt from Federal taxes.

Investment income may be subject to certain state and local taxes and, depending on your tax status, the federal Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT). Capital Gains are not exempt from state and federal income tax.

There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Fund shares are not guaranteed by the U.S. Government.

Even though the fund is designed to purchase assets exempt from federal taxes, there is no guarantee that all of the fund’s income will be exempt from federal income tax or the federal alternative minimum tax(AMT). Specifically, the portfolio managers are permitted at any time to invest up to 20% of the fund’s assets in debt securities with interest payments that are subject to federal income tax and/or federal AMT.

The S&P Intermediate Term National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index includes all bonds in the National index that have an effective maturity of 1 month up to 20 years. Bonds issued by U.S. territories including Puerto Rico, are excluded from this index. Non-FDIC Insured | May Lose Value | No Bank Guarantee

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities(Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities(ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points(BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for“carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations(CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations(CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities(CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index(CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration(CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or“cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets(EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund(ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee(FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve(Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts(Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation(GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product(GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises(GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield(HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade(IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A“market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI(All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of

U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into"pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures(PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing(QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts(REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard“swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities(TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct"full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes(VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any"inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Total Return Breakouts: 3 Tariff Trades As China, Value, And Financial Stocks Move Higher
data.description:
Introduction

The unpredictability of noise traders' beliefs creates a risk in the price of the asset that deters rational arbitragers from aggressively betting against them. As a result, prices can diverge significantly from fundamental values even in the absence of fundamental risk.

~ DeLong J. B. et al. (1990).

I have spent the last 35 years trading, researching, and constructing algorithms to identify and leverage the value across fundamental, technical, and behavioral finance models. Of the ten portfolio models designed for optimal portfolio mixes for members to beat the market at Value & Momentum Breakouts, eight come from enhancing well-tested anomaly research in published financial journals.

If you believe like I do, that there is much more to stock trading than just buy, hold, and hope that every future event works out perfectly as promised for your stock – then these trading models are for you. This article highlights some of our recent top trades from three very different portfolio models, ranging from aggressive short-term weekly breakouts to long-term growth and value portfolios with high dividends.

Three Trades from Different Portfolio Models

In this article, I provide another sampling of how we use the optimal portfolio models to identify long-term continued strength, short-term aggressive breakout stocks, and even apply timing models to long-term value stocks for the best results. As I repeatedly tell our investment community, it is important to not only diversify your stock selections, but it is also critical to be where the market fund flows are improving for the best returns. As many long-term traders know well, it is very rare for one type of investment to lead in consecutive years. More details in my 2025 Market Forecast article explain the models and process. I am certain again this year that we will see more rotation and if you are just riding the buy-and-hold approach, you can find yourself in a painful situation.

Chart 1. The NDR chart below shows the fewest number of S&P 500 (SP500) stocks outperforming the S&P 500 index the last two years since 1998/99.

This article builds on many prior Total Return Breakout articles and emphasizes the extreme volatility this year, especially in the Technology sector:

  • Total Return Breakouts: Records, Skew, And All New Portfolios For 2025
  • 2025 Market Forecast Article.

Let me briefly introduce a sample of stocks and funds from three different portfolios. The sample selections this week come from our long-term Piotroski-Graham Value portfolio, Negative Forensic Value portfolio and Sector Gauge ETF breakout tracker. These selections represent a small sample from among 10 different portfolio models shared in our investing group at regular weekly, monthly, and annual intervals depending on the portfolio type. Next, I will explain the value selection process and the unique overlap with current momentum breakout conditions. Lastly, (if you haven't realized it already) I will share why timing matters greatly, even in our value investing portfolios for long-term growth.

  • FinVolution Group (FINV)
  • UP Fintech Holding Limited (TIGR)
  • Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF (YINN).

Three Selections in the Positive Signal

First, We Have the FinVolution Group ADR

FinVolution comes from our Piotroski-Graham Value portfolio, intended for two-year measurement returns following the scholars’ same two-year test period. This is an exclusively fundamental finance selection based on many key value ratios considered in two different scholarly models separated by decades in their published research. The proven Buy-and-Hold returns across the full 2-year timeline are well documented, but we know well that timing matters greatly, and signals can improve all your trading.

In the long-term value portfolio, we leverage published financial algorithms from scholars like Benjamin Graham and Joseph Piotroski, who have shown strong returns in the published literature. I have combined their two algorithms and enhanced them further to release these Piotroski-Graham value portfolios two times a year, in July and January.

What is unique about FinVolution since its selection in our July mid-year Piotroski value portfolio is the high frequency of the extreme Segment 6 positive MDA breakout signals since last year. Our MDA methodology explained in a webinar here on Seeking Alpha uses fundamental, behavioral, and technical variables to signal enhanced breakout time periods.

The excellent valuations of 7.22x earnings and 0.69x sales with no debt and strong buying momentum support continue price growth to much higher levels in the coming weeks. While FINV is already up 75% from July, it still offers a good 2.9% dividend along with excellent value to retest prior highs back to at least 10/share from 2021.

Second, We Have UP Fintech Holding Limited

UP Fintech comes from the January 2025 Negative Forensic value portfolio.

I get plenty of questions about this model that selects the most negative scoring stocks using four of the top forensic algorithms in the financial literature. One of the most prominent negative forensic stocks was Enron, and for many years the company was one of the best-performing stocks in the market. In the 3 years before any irregularities were explained to be the most extreme kind of irregularities, the stock gained over 600%. Companies that have these negative scores can take steps to normalize the forensic ratios, or address the red flags that these accounting ratios represent for a wide variety of reasons well beyond the scope of this article.

Negative scores do not necessarily confirm anything bad about the stock, but highlight fundamental ratios that are well outside normal accounting ranges. Such negative extremes in the algorithms can actually reveal a positive result from unusual changes. I will not go into much detail on the algorithms here, but if that field of financial analysis interests you, then consider my forensic analysis on Infosys (INFY) or on Super Micro Computer (SMCI) regarding the recent resignation of the E&Y accounting firm:

  • Super Micro Computer And Forensic Flags To Consider After E&Y Resignation
  • US Fraud examiner says forensic algorithms show no evidence of financial wrongdoing at Infosys.

Both the Positive and Negative Forensic portfolios are released twice each year here on Seeking Alpha since 2018 with strong annual results.

A few strong characteristics of the Negative Forensic stocks are the much higher levels of volatility and extremely high frequency of delisting from mergers, acquisitions, or failure to maintain required listing standards of the exchanges. You can see the volatility in the chart above with many periods of extreme positive signals preceding considerable price movements followed then by large declines.

Sector Momentum Gauges

The sector and market gauges give us a unique way to use the MDA charts to identify the most positive sectors for large gains in various bull or bear ETF/ETN funds. A current fund to highlight is the recent China bull fund breakout of Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF, even as rising tariffs are expected to impact China trade.

Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF

The current breakout is crossing above a key resistance level for February as large market inflows are flooding into Chinese stocks. Part of this momentum comes from the declining value of the dollar from the January peak at the highest levels since November 2022. Lower dollar values tend to benefit financial stocks, emerging markets, and especially commodities like gold priced in dollars.

This dollar trade that correlates strongly with emerging markets and commodities like Gold is illustrated in the Basic Material sector gauge weekly chart below. The current signal is positive since mid-January and shown using (UYM) ProShares Ultra Materials in yellow. Other Basic Material managed ETFs may correlate better with the signals between the red/green momentum gauge crossovers, but Ultra Materials is also highly positive +13.55% YTD.

FINRA cautions investors that:

Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that offer leverage or that are designed to perform inversely to the index or benchmark they track-or both-are growing in number and popularity. While such products may be useful in some sophisticated trading strategies, they are highly complex financial instruments that are typically designed to achieve their stated objectives on a daily basis. Due to the effects of compounding, their performance over longer periods of time can differ significantly from their stated daily objective. Therefore, inverse and leveraged ETFs that are reset daily typically are unsuitable for retail investors who plan to hold them for longer than one trading session, particularly in volatile markets.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Market Outlook

While our portfolios go out in fixed weekly, monthly and semi-annual schedules in our investment group regardless of market conditions, we can still benefit from market signals. Experience has shown that fighting against the MDA market variables can be very adverse to our returns. The market really doesn’t care how good we think of our selected stocks or market valuations, and it is wise to trade accordingly.

The weekly S&P 500 gauges are still negative as we continue through Q4 earnings, with mega giants Walmart (WMT) and Nvidia (NVDA) still left to report. We are watching to see if more sectors can turn positive next week.

Conclusion

Scholars have documented for years that, “harvesting different risk baskets, each with a premium that has low-to-negative correlations to the others” is an excellent way to produce strong risk-adjusted returns. These ongoing sector rotations and signals continue to give us many new opportunities for profit in the years ahead as we build our optimal portfolio mixes each year.

As I detailed in prior articles, I am focused on a rotation to value this year that may greatly benefit out-of-favor stocks over prior momentum gainers.

The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) suggests more declines for the dollar ahead that may greatly benefit emerging markets and commodities priced in dollars. Conversely, a rising dollar tends to be worse for trade and the broader markets.

This article shares a few ways to detect strong opportunities early and leverage unique statistical models published in financial literature for our benefit. You may be able to leverage our selections and signals even better than I do.

I wish you well in all your trading decisions!

JD Henning, PhD.

References

J. Bradford DeLong, Andrei Shleifer, Lawrence Summers, and Robert Waldmann. “Noise Trader Risk in Financial Markets,” Journal of Political Economy 98 (1990). 703-738.

data.title: CVS Health: One Good Quarter Is Not Enough; Retain Sell
data.description:
Performance Assessment

In mid-Dec'24, I retained my 'Sell' view on CVS Health (NYSE:CVS). This was unfortunate as the stock has rallied hard since then:

As a result, a lot of the active return generated since my initiating coverage's 'Sell' view on CVS has been eroded away, although the stock has still lagged the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO) since:

Thesis

CVS Health reported Q4 FY24 results last week. Upon review, I see arguments that favor both the bulls and the bears. But overall, I am inclined to still retain a bearish outlook on the stock:

  1. Q4 results delivery suggests that the market had been too pessimistic.
  2. Q4 saw yet another quarter of margin erosion in the insurance business.
  3. But profitability pressures may ease in FY25.
  4. Transparent pricing via CostVantage may boost volumes in the pharmacy business.
  5. Valuations are at a lower than usual discount vs peers.
  6. Relative technicals outlook is still bearish amid a likely bull trap.
  7. High leverage levels are a key risk monitorable.

Q4 results delivery suggests that the market had been too pessimistic

CVS delivered a weak and less than usual revenue beat (+0.9%) vs median consensus estimates:

I think a contributing factor of the weaker than usual performance was the fact that membership in the health care benefits (insurance) portfolio started to drop in advance of 2025 rate increases:

Medical membership of approximately 27.1 million was roughly flat sequentially as membership in our Individual Exchange business started to modestly decline in advance of the 2025 rate increases we took in that book.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

But the biggest and most unexpected surprise was a 35bps beat on EBIT margins:

This is meaningful when we put things in context; CVS' biggest issue in recent quarters has been declining profitability. So margin performance that beat expectations despite headwinds continuing (discussed more in the following section) suggests that the market may have been too pessimistic on the stock. I think this has been the driving factor of the +20% rally in the stock post the Q4 FY24 earnings release.

Q4 saw yet another quarter of margin erosion in the insurance business

On a TTM basis, CVS' health care benefits (insurance) business makes up 35% and 32% of company-wide revenues and gross margins, respectively. But it has a mere 2.7% of adjusted EBIT, with TTM EBIT margins of only 0.2%. Despite higher than expected profitability performance, CVS' issues in its health care benefits (insurance) business has seen no end to steady gross margin erosion:

This has been driven mostly by higher medical benefit ratios (MBR), which is a measure of health insurance business' profitability:

MBR is calculated by taking insurance costs that occur above the gross profit line and dividing that by insurance premium revenue.

Q4's rise in MBR was driven by a suite of factors:

This increase was primarily driven by higher utilization, the premium impact of lower Star ratings for payment year 2024 and higher acuity in Medicaid.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

It looks like Wall St analysts expect these pressures to continue, as they have generally downgraded EPS estimates for most of FY25:

Notwithstanding that, we should note that there are some elements in play that could ease profitability pressures this year.

But profitability pressures may ease in FY25

Firstly, CVS' insurance business will be benefiting from better insurance policy terms in 2025:

We are cautiously optimistic that our benefit design changes in 2025 will help alleviate some of the supplemental benefit pressure… During the quarter, we made additional progress on rate updates, and with over 40% of our book having repriced in early January 2025, we have a line of sight to a mid-4 percentage point rate increase. Our overall rate advocacy efforts are currently on track.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

Secondly, management expects to end FY25 with a high single-digit decline in Medicare Advantage memberships:

We also expect our Medicare Advantage membership to end the year down a high single-digit percentage from year-end 2024… reflects strong execution by our teams as they made the difficult choices necessary to improve Medicare Advantage profitability in 2025.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

This is important because Medicare Advantage members make up a decent 16.4% of overall CVS insurance members. And it is the least profitable membership group for CVS due to higher incidences of higher cost claims. From an EBIT perspective, it has been posting losses in the -4.5% to -5% range. But with changes in policy terms and an expected decline in the Medicare Advantage member counts, the management team is looking to move toward 3% to 5% EBIT margins in this business, enabled via a 100bps improvement in MBRs to 91.5%.

This is the bullish side of the story. However, I am wary of being too quick to believe this narrative as the company's execution record has been poor in FY24, with multiple cuts to its forecasts.

Transparent pricing via CostVantage may boost volumes in the pharmacy business

On a TTM basis, CVS' pharmacy business makes up 33%, 48% and 42% of revenues, gross profits and adjusted EBIT respectively. So this is another key driver of the stock's performance.

Now, CVS has a new drug pricing called CostVantage, which aims to simplify and make drug pricing more transparent based on its underlying cost, a defined markup and a dispensing fee to reflect value in the pharmacy service provided. It has been rolled out to all commercial prescriptions as of 6 Jan'25. I see this as a tailwind to prescription volumes growth in the pharmacy business:

In FY25, I expect high single-digit growth going ahead in prescription volumes filled.

Valuations are at a lower than usual discount vs peers

CVS is trading at a 1-yr fwd PE of 11.2x, which is just a 1.1% discount to the comps' median of 11.3x. At first glance, this may indicate fair valuation. However, note that CVS has usually traded at a 27.9% discount to its comps:

So a 1.1% discount is relatively much higher than average. Hence, I would say that CVS stock is trading at a relatively higher than usual premium vs its comps.

Relative technicals outlook is still bearish amid a likely bull trap

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of CVS vs SPX500

CVS vs SPX500 shows a strong incumbent downtrend that has seen 2 months of a sharp bullish correction. The ratio prices right now are right near the monthly resistance level. I note that there has been minimal sideways rollover or consolidation, which is typical in trend reversals according to Wyckoff theory. Hence, I am inclined to believe the recent move up is more likely to be a bull trap and that there is some more downside and hence underperformance vs the S&P500 to follow.

High leverage levels are a key risk monitorable

Whilst much of the focus on CVS may be on its profitability profile, I think we should also keep on eye on its leverage levels:

As profitability has been pressured, CVS' net debt/EBITDA has been steadily rising to 4.7x on a TTM basis as of the Dec'24 quarter. I think this is quite an elevated level for a business like CVS. So this is a key risk to monitor for the bulls.

Takeaway & Positioning

Since my last update on the stock in Dec'24, CVS has rallied 45%, which includes a ~20% rally after the Q4 FY24 earnings release last week. This has eroded a lot of the initial alpha generated from my first 'Sell' view on the stock in Oct'24. I think the reaction to the Q4 results may have been due to an EBIT margins beat, which is a surprise positive, even though the company continued to see margin erosion in its insurance business. I acknowledge that in FY25 there are positive catalysts for profitability improvement here, driven by more favorable policy rates for CVS' insurance business as well as reduced membership numbers from Medicare Advantage; the least profitable membership group for CVS. However, I am cautious in believing the margin expansion story right now as the company has an underwhelming track record in execution, having cut its forecasts multiple times in FY24. Another positive I acknowledge is that on the pharmacy business side, I believe the transparent CostVantage pricing model change may help boost prescription volumes.

But considering a lower than usual discount valuation vs its comps, a likely bull trap in the relative technicals vs the S&P500 and rising leverage levels, I choose to retain my 'Sell' view.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
